name: Eta H1-over-TLS handshake latency under concurrent load

description: Baseline: Eta H1-over-TLS handshake latency under concurrent load (oha --disable-keepalive, c=16, n=2000, median of 3). p50 2.69ms; handshake = ECDHE-RSA RSA-2048 sign serialized on single core.

asi.hypothesis: baseline of Eta HTTPS (H1-over-TLS) handshake latency under concurrent load (oha --disable-keepalive, c=16, fresh TLS handshake per request, median of 3)
asi.diagnosis: The broad-suite H1 TLS ~15-16ms p99 is entirely TLS handshake cost. Full handshake = ECDHE-RSA with RSA-2048 server signature (~1.4ms CPU); at c=16 they serialize on the single-core Eio server (p50 2.69ms). Steady-state H1 TLS request latency is already fine (~0.2ms). TCP_NODELAY already set; Tls_eio context built once per listener.
asi.baseline: h1_tls_hs_p50_us=2689us, hs_p99=8668us, hs_rps=4692, ka_p99=45269us (noisy n=1000 keep-alive symptom), peak_rss=51MB
asi.levers: 1) TLS session resumption/tickets (skip RSA sign) -- verify oha resumes. 2) Remove per-handshake with_timeout fork in run_https_connection. 3) Parallelize CPU-bound handshakes across domains (attacks c=16 serialization). 4) TLS1.3+X25519 fast path. 5) mirage-crypto RSA backend.
asi.next: Start with the cheapest structural win: remove the per-handshake with_timeout sleeper fork in run_https_connection (same anti-pattern that gave H1 plain -45%). Then investigate handshake parallelism across domains (the c=16 serialization is the biggest multiplier).

description: Baseline (multi-domain, 8 domains): Eta H1-over-TLS handshake throughput. hs_p50 1.85ms, hs_p99 7.1ms, rps 6694, RSS 214MB. p50 ~10x the 0.17ms per-handshake CPU = shared-state contention across domains.

asi.baseline: multi-domain (8 domains, server cores 4-19, oha core 3): hs_p50=1851us, hs_p99=7129us, hs_rps=6694, ka_p99=13777us, peak_rss=214MB
asi.config_change: Enabled multi-domain HTTPS accept via ETA_SERVER_DOMAINS=8 (eta_server.start -> start_https ~domain_manager ~domain_policy:Additional 8). This is a harness/deployment config, not a counted library opt. Single-domain 1-core baseline (p50 2689us) archived.
asi.diagnosis: Per-handshake CPU ~0.17ms (OpenSSL RSA-2048, already fast). p50 1.85ms at 8 domains = ~10x non-linear -> handshakes serialize on shared state. Suspects: global t.mutex per-connection bookkeeping (register_pending_tls/register_transitioned_connection/record_tls_handshake/unregister, ~5 lock ops/conn across all domains); Cstruct.create 32768 per feed_bio + Cstruct.create pending per drain_bio (bigarray malloc churn contending across domains); OpenSSL global locks/RNG.
asi.rss_warning: 8 domains -> 214MB RSS (vs 51MB single). More domains cost memory; io_uring memlock is 8MB so domain count is capped ~16.
asi.hypothesis: baseline of multi-domain HTTPS handshake throughput
asi.next: First counted experiment: reuse per-connection feed/drain buffers in tls_eio.ml instead of Cstruct.create 32768 per feed_bio / Cstruct.create pending per drain_bio -- cut bigarray malloc/free churn that contends across domains during the handshake burst.

description: TLS handshake: reuse per-connection feed/drain BIO scratch buffers instead of Cstruct.create per feed_bio/drain_bio call. p50 -31.7%, RSS -23%.

asi.hypothesis: feed_bio allocated Cstruct.create 32768 per call and drain_bio Cstruct.create pending per call (several per handshake). Reuse per-connection feed_buf (32KB) + growable drain_buf (16KB), serialized by read/write mutexes. Cuts bigarray malloc/free churn that contends in the allocator across domains.
asi.result: p50 1850.84->1264.27 (-31.7%), hs_p99 7129->6192 (-13%), ka_p99 13777->11394 (-17%), RSS 219MB->169MB (-23%), rps flat. Bigger than expected -- bigarray malloc/free + GC pressure was a real per-handshake cost under multi-domain load.
asi.validation: all H1+shared unit suites pass. Safety: feed_buf reused under read_mutex (feed_bio serialized), drain_buf under write_mutex (drain_bio serialized); both per-connection (not shared across domains), so no cross-domain aliasing. OpenSSL bio_write/bio_read copy into/out of the BIO, and Flow.write is synchronous, so the scratch buffer is free to reuse after each op. drain_buf grows if a pending record exceeds capacity.
asi.next: p50 still 1264us ~= 7x the 0.17ms CPU -- remaining cost is per-round-trip Eio scheduling + the handshake I/O loop. Next: reduce handshake round-trip/scheduling overhead (e.g. coalesce drain_bio writes, examine the feed/drain mutex churn), or check OpenSSL SSL_CTX/session-cache global locks contending across domains.

description: TLS server CTX: SSL_SESS_CACHE_SERVER -> SSL_SESS_CACHE_OFF to drop the global per-handshake session-cache lock that serializes handshakes across domains. hs_p99 -11%, rps +4% (p50 noise-bound).

asi.hypothesis: SSL_CTX_set_session_cache_mode(SSL_SESS_CACHE_SERVER) takes a global ctx->lock write-lock on every full handshake (to store the session), serializing handshakes across Eio domains. Switch to SSL_SESS_CACHE_OFF; TLS1.3/1.2 resumption uses stateless tickets so real-client resumption is preserved.
asi.noise_finding: CRITICAL: hs_p50 is noisy (~+-25%, band ~1250-1850us under multi-domain). #11's 1264 was a lucky low sample -- re-running #11's committed state gives ~1750-1850. #11's RSS win (-23%) IS real/consistent. The stable signals are hs_rps, hs_p99, RSS -- NOT hs_p50. Must stabilize the metric next.
asi.result: cache-OFF two runs: p50 1847, 1755 (noise); hs_p99 5759/6310 (vs baseline 7129, improved); rps 6675/6992 (vs 6694, up); RSS ~171MB. p50 within noise of baseline; p99+rps consistently better.
asi.next: STABILIZE THE HARNESS before more experiments: bump HS_N (->6000) + give oha enough cores (it caps ~6700/s on one core, confounding throughput) + raise concurrency to actually saturate the 8 server domains so contention fixes show. Consider switching primary to hs_rps (throughput under saturation, more stable) or keep p50 but with far more samples.
asi.validation: all unit suites pass. Security preserved: no cipher/version change, resumption still available via stateless tickets; only the in-process session-ID cache (an optimization) is disabled. Correct design for multi-domain TLS.

name: Eta H1-over-TLS handshake throughput (multi-domain, stable harness)
